topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor smoothed the crumpled permission slip on the kitchen counter for the tenth time. Her gaze kept drifting to the photo album on the shelf, a collection of faded school pictures. Then, she saw it: a picture of a small child, perhaps four years old, beaming under a bright yellow sun, standing next to a woman with kind, knowing eyes. A sudden, cold knot tightened in Eleanor’s stomach. She walked slowly toward the living room, her steps heavy. When she finally looked at the current school roster, the name etched beside the class she was currently enrolling her son, Leo, into—Ms. Albright—made the air catch in her throat. Her breath hitched. She reread the name, then looked back at the photograph, a slow, disbelieving shake running through her shoulders.

[story 2]

"Are you sure about this, honey?" Mark asked, watching his daughter pace the hallway. She stopped abruptly, turning to face him, her eyes wide, pupils dilated. "I just... I can't believe it." Mark watched her face, noting the slight tremor in her hands as she gripped her backpack straps. She looked down at the laminated schedule in her hand, then back up at him, her mouth slightly agape. "Ms. Albright," she whispered, the sound barely audible. "She taught me. She taught me everything." Mark felt a strange, heavy stillness settle over him. He simply nodded, the realization of the shared history washing over him, leaving him momentarily speechless.

[story 3]

The administrative office felt strangely quiet. Sarah clutched the enrollment forms, her knuckles white where she gripped the edges of the paper. When the principal finally handed her the updated schedule, her eyes scanned the list until they landed on the familiar handwriting next to the designation for Leo’s class: Ms. Albright. A sharp intake of breath escaped her. Her jaw went slack. She blinked rapidly, trying to process the image before her. A slow, almost involuntary exhale escaped her lips. She felt a sudden, dizzying lurch in her chest, as if the floor had tilted beneath her feet. "No," she managed to articulate, the word thin and reedy. "That can't be right."
